In the starter code, the function is minStartingIndex. So:

class Solution {
public:
    int minStartingIndex(string s, string pattern) {
        int m = pattern.length();
        int n = s.length();
        for (int i = 0; i <= n - m; ++i) {
            int diff = 0;
            for (int j = 0; j < m; ++j) {
                if (s[i + j] != pattern[j]) {
                    ++diff;
                    if (diff > 1) break;
                }
            }
            if (diff <= 1) return i;
        }
        return -1;
    }
};

But this is what the current code already does. For each i, during the check, as soon as two mismatches are found, it breaks and moves on.

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int minStartingIndex(string s, string pattern) {
        int m = pattern.length();
        int n = s.length();
        for (int i = 0; i <= n - m; ++i) {
            int diff = 0;
            for (int j = 0; j < m; ++j) {
                if (s[i + j] != pattern[j]) {
                    ++diff;
                    if (diff > 1) break;
                }
            }
            if (diff <= 1) return i;
        }
        return -1;
    }
};
```
efficiency: MEDIUM
time complexity: O((n - m + 1) * m)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a brute-force sliding window approach with early termination. For each starting index in the string, it checks character-by-character against the pattern, stopping as soon as more than one mismatch is found. While this is efficient for small patterns, it degrades to O(n*m) in worst-case scenarios. The main weakness is poor scalability for large patterns, but the early termination helps in practice.
